preFix.title: Watercress soup
preFix.subtitle: Peppery, vivid green, and ready quickly
preFix.excerpt: Fresh watercress blended into a potato-thickened stock with a finish of crème fraîche. The key is keeping the cooking time to a minimum once the watercress goes in, which keeps the colour vivid and the peppery flavour sharp.
preFix.sourceNotes: Watercress has been cultivated in British chalk streams, particularly in Hampshire, since the 19th century. As a soup it became a fixture of British restaurant menus in the 1980s new-wave period.

content.text: Watercress soup has one rule: do not overcook the watercress. Once it goes into the hot stock it needs no more than two minutes before it goes into the blender. Any longer and the

that gives the soup its colour starts to break down, turning vivid green to muddy brown. The potato added earlier gives the body and creaminess; the watercress provides the flavour and the colour. Speed is the technique.
Melt {{unsalted-butter}} of butter in a large saucepan over a medium-low heat. Add the {{onion}} onion and cook for 6 minutes until soft. Add the {{garlic}} of garlic and cook for 1 minute.
Add the {{potato}} of diced potato and pour in {{stock-vegetable}} of hot stock. Bring to a simmer and cook for 12 minutes until the potato is completely tender.
Add the {{watercress}} of watercress to the simmering soup. Cook for exactly 2 minutes. Do not let it go longer.
Transfer to a jug blender immediately and blend until completely smooth. Stir in the {{creme-fraiche}} of crème fraîche and the juice of {{lemon}} lemon. Taste and season with salt.
Ladle into bowls and add a small spoonful of crème fraîche to each. Serve immediately.

items.fix: Two minutes is the limit once the watercress hits the hot stock. To rescue a dull-coloured soup, blend a handful of raw watercress leaves with a little cold water and stir in ,  this boosts the colour back.
items.cause: The watercress was cooked for too long before blending.
items.symptom: Soup is khaki or brownish, not green

items.fix: Strip leaves from any stalk thicker than a matchstick before adding. Thin stalks are fine. Adding the lemon juice also cuts bitterness.
items.cause: The thick stalks were left on. They carry more bitterness than the leaves.

items.fix: Use a jug blender and blend for at least 60 seconds. Pass through a fine-mesh sieve if you want a refined, completely smooth result.
items.cause: A stick blender does not handle watercress stalks well.
items.symptom: Texture has strands or is not smooth

items.fix: Add a handful of raw watercress leaves to the blender alongside the cooked soup. The raw leaves give more punch.
items.cause: Supermarket watercress is often milder than wild-grown. Heat also softens the peppery bite.

content.text: Watercress has been eaten in Britain since Roman times. It grows wild in the chalk streams of Hampshire and Dorset, and commercial cultivation began in the Itchen and Test valleys in the early 19th century. As a blended soup, it became a fixture of British restaurants in the 1980s, when lighter cooking replaced the heavier soups of earlier decades.
